title: Queue and Deque: ArrayDeque, PriorityQueue
description: Processing elements in a specific order -- FIFO with Queue, both ends with Deque, and priority order with PriorityQueue.
objectives: Use ArrayDeque for double-ended operations, including as a stack

sections.text: A List (List: ArrayList vs LinkedList) lets you access any position freely. A Queue is more restrictive on purpose: elements are added at one end and removed from the other, in the exact order they arrived -- like a real-world line at a checkout counter.
A collection that processes elements in first-in-first-out (FIFO) order -- the first element added is the first one removed.

sections.title: Common Queue methods
rows: Adds x to the back of the queue
Removes and returns the element at the front
Returns the front element WITHOUT removing it

sections.text: Queue is declared here using LinkedList as the concrete implementation -- LinkedList (List: ArrayList vs LinkedList) implements both List and Queue/Deque, which is why it fits here too.
A double-ended queue supporting insertion and removal at BOTH ends efficiently -- ArrayDeque is the standard general-purpose implementation.

push()/pop() give Deque last-in-first-out (LIFO) behavior -- the OPPOSITE order from Queue's offer()/poll(). Java's official recommendation is to use ArrayDeque (via Deque) instead of the older java.util.Stack class for stack behavior -- we only preview this here, since Stacks get their own full module (Stacks & Queues) soon.
A queue that always removes the SMALLEST (by default) element first, regardless of insertion order, rather than following strict FIFO order.

Even though 30 was added FIRST, poll() returns 10 first -- PriorityQueue completely ignores insertion order, always giving you the current smallest element. This makes it fundamentally different from a plain Queue, despite sharing the same offer()/poll() method names. PriorityQueue gets its own full lesson in the upcoming Heap module, since it's Java's built-in heap implementation.

sections.title: A very common DSA pattern
points: Every ( pushes onto the stack -- "I'm still waiting for a matching )"
Every ) pops one ( off, if any exist -- a ) with nothing to match means unbalanced immediately
At the end, the stack must be completely empty for everything to have matched -- this exact pattern reappears in the Stack / Monotonic Stack lessons

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing Queue's FIFO order (offer/poll) with Deque-as-stack's LIFO order (push/pop) -- they're opposite processing orders.
Assuming PriorityQueue processes elements in insertion order like a plain Queue -- it always gives the smallest element first, regardless of when it was added.
Forgetting to check stack.isEmpty() before popping in patterns like balanced-parentheses checking, risking an exception on an empty stack.
Using the older java.util.Stack class out of habit, when ArrayDeque (via Deque) is the modern, recommended choice.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does FIFO mean, and which collection embodies it?
questions.answer: First-In-First-Out -- the first element added is the first one removed. Queue embodies this with offer() (add to back) and poll() (remove from front).

questions.question: What is a Deque, and how can it act as both a queue and a stack?
questions.answer: A Deque (double-ended queue) supports insertion and removal at both ends. Using addLast/removeFirst gives FIFO (queue) behavior, while push/pop gives LIFO (stack) behavior -- ArrayDeque is the standard general-purpose implementation for both.

questions.question: How does PriorityQueue's ordering differ from a regular Queue's?
questions.answer: A regular Queue processes strictly in insertion order (FIFO). PriorityQueue ignores insertion order entirely and always removes the current smallest element first.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Queue processes elements in FIFO order using offer() (add to back) and poll() (remove from front) -- like a real checkout line. Deque, typically implemented with ArrayDeque, supports efficient insertion and removal at both ends, and can act as either a queue (addLast/removeFirst) or a stack (push/pop, LIFO order) -- the modern recommended replacement for the older java.util.Stack class. PriorityQueue looks similar syntactically but always removes the smallest element first regardless of insertion order, making it Java's built-in heap, covered fully in the Heap module. A very common DSA pattern using Deque-as-stack is checking balanced parentheses, which reappears in the Stack / Monotonic Stack lessons.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Queue = a checkout LINE -- first in line, first served (FIFO). Deque-as-stack = a stack of PLATES -- last one placed on top comes off first (LIFO). PriorityQueue = a hospital ER -- most urgent (smallest) goes first, no matter when they arrived.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What does FIFO mean, and which Java collection implements it?
How can a Deque behave like a stack?
How does PriorityQueue's processing order differ from a regular Queue's?
What's the modern recommended replacement for java.util.Stack?
